question.text: Which of the following is a limitation common to hedging with exchange-traded derivatives across ALL asset classes (currency, interest rate, and equity)?

answer.text: Standardized contract specifications (lot size, expiry dates, contract months) may not perfectly match the hedger's exposure, creating residual risk
Exchange-traded derivatives are only available for the top 10 most traded assets
Hedging with exchange-traded derivatives always results in a loss for the hedger
Exchange-traded derivatives cannot be used by corporate entities, only by individual traders

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Exchange-traded derivatives have standardized specifications — fixed lot sizes, predetermined expiry dates, and limited contract months. A hedger whose exposure doesn't match these specifications (e.g., odd lot size, exposure date between expiry dates) will have an imperfect hedge with residual risk. **The Trap:** ETDs are available for many assets, not just top 10. Hedging can result in either gain or loss (it locks in a rate). Both corporates and individuals can use ETDs. **Cross-Exam Context:** This limitation is discussed across all three series (I, IV, VIII). It's universal to exchange-traded derivatives regardless of asset class. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** OTC derivatives can provide exact customization (size, expiry, terms), but at the cost of counterparty risk. ETDs trade standardized but have exchange guarantee — choose based on your priority.
